mappings.rationaleAI: The An Lushan Rebellion (755-763) and its devastating impact on the Tang Dynasty, including loss of control of northern China, enormous death toll, and effects on governance, directly corresponds to A.1.a on the late T'ang and the rebellion's effects.

Genghis Khan's conquest of the Chin (1211-34) and establishment of Mongol rule, the Yasa legal code, and Pax Mongolica trade directly correspond to B.1.a on Genghis Khan's conquest of the Chin and establishment of the Yuan dynasty.

Zheng He's Ming Dynasty maritime voyages (1405-1433) across Southeast Asia, India, and East Africa, and their abrupt cessation under Confucian isolationism, directly correspond to C.1.b on developments in foreign relations and economic policy under the Ming.

The Great Wall's major reconstruction under the Ming dynasty against Mongol threats corresponds to C.1.a-b on Ming political structure and foreign relations, and to the broader theme of frontier defence across Chinese dynasties.
The resurgence of Neo-Confucianism during the Sung period is directly relevant to A.3 on Sung cultural developments, specifically the philosophical revival that shaped Chinese intellectual life.

Romance of the Three Kingdoms, attributed to Luo Guanzhong (14th century), is one of the Four Great Classical Novels and reflects Ming cultural life (C.1.c), while its historical setting connects to broader Chinese literary traditions.

The Han Synthesis as the intellectual framework for two millennia provides essential background for understanding Neo-Confucianism's resurgence during the Sung period (A.3), particularly the examination system and Confucian state ideology.
